channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

  5. Enzyme engineers tackle protein damage from aging  C&EN

item.title: Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the solar system, with hundreds of active volcanoes that can shoot sulphur plumes more than 500 kilometres above its surface — a landscape so extreme that early Voyager scientists initially mistook the p - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the solar system, with hundreds of active volcanoes that can shoot sulphur plumes more than 500 kilometres above its surface — a landscape so extreme that early Voyager scientists initially mistook the p  Space Daily

  3. What lurks beneath the volcanoes of Jupiter's moon Io? NASA's Juno probe just took a peek  Space
  4. Gravitational Tug-of-War Turns up the Heat on Jupiter's Moon Io, Causing Underground Temperatures to Soar  Discover Magazine

item.title: Skylab cost roughly $2.2 billion, hosted nine astronauts across three missions and remained in orbit for just over six years — before breaking apart over Western Australia, where its remains became museum exhibits, Lucite-encased souvenirs and, according t - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Skylab cost roughly $2.2 billion, hosted nine astronauts across three missions and remained in orbit for just over six years — before breaking apart over Western Australia, where its remains became museum exhibits, Lucite-encased souvenirs and, according t  Space Daily
  2. NASA's first space station weighed 77 tons and its launch nearly destroyed it, but engineers saved the thing with an improvised parasol, and when it finally fell out of orbit in 1979 a small Australian town fined the agency $400 for littering.  19FortyFive
  3. NASA's space station went out of orbit and dropped tons of debris over Australia 45 years ago: learn the story of Skylab, the first US space station that weighed 77 tons and turned a predicted disaster into a lesson for all space exploration.  CPG Click Oil and Gas

  4. Faint new planet is revealed around a young star after a decade in hiding  AP News
  5. JWST Found Beta Pictoris d by Reading Its Atmosphere, Not Photographing It  Tech Times

item.title: Finding no evidence of life in Enceladus’s ocean could be almost as scientifically important as finding it — because a world with liquid water, internal heat, phosphorus and complex organic chemistry but no detectable biology would suggest that the ingredient - Space Daily

item.description:

item.title: A Wild Parasite Makes Ants Live 10x Longer And Tricks Others Into Worshipping Them - ScienceAlert
item.description:

  2. Tapeworm hijacks worker ants, giving them queen-like metabolism and longer lives  Phys.org
  3. Tapeworm discovered that makes its host live 10 times longer and prompts them to receive ‘royal treatment’  The Independent
  4. A worker ant of the species Temnothorx nylanderi infected with the tapeworm Anomotaenia brevis, recognizable by its yellowish coloration, alongside an uninfected worker  EurekAlert!
  5. A tapeworm infects worker ants and makes them live several times longer by turning their bodies more like those of queens  The Times of India

item.title: A fossil remained trapped inside a Japanese museum rock for 30 years. Scientists now think it may belong - The Times of India

item.description: Vulcan Centaur swapped out again: NASA goes with SpaceX Falcon Heavy rocket for launch of space weather mission  Space

item.title: Saturn's moon Titan is so large it outsizes the planet Mercury, making it the second-biggest moon in the solar system after Jupiter's Ganymede - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Saturn's moon Titan is so large it outsizes the planet Mercury, making it the second-biggest moon in the solar system after Jupiter's Ganymede  Space Daily
  2. Titan has rivers, rainfall, lakes and a slow hydrological cycle exactly like Earth's, except that every drop of it is liquid methane and the rock the rivers run over is water ice  spacewar.com
  3. There is a moon of Saturn where the rain is essentially liquid natural gas, the mountains are made of water frozen so hard it behaves like rock, and the lakes at the north pole contain more combustible fuel than every proven oil and gas reserve on Earth combin  ScienceBlog.com
  4. Saturn's moon Titan has rivers, lakes and rainfall without a single drop of water, and scientists know why  The Times of India
  5. On Saturn's moon Titan, molecules that chemistry says should never mix are mixing — NASA and Chalmers University researchers found that at minus 183 degrees Celsius, substances as incompatible as water and oil can dissolve into each other  Space Daily

item.title: To make a single pound of honey, the bees in a hive may visit around two million flowers and can fly more than 50,000 miles between them — equivalent to more than twice around the planet - Space Daily

item.title: Scientists think at least two of Uranus's moons — Titania and Oberon — may harbour liquid water oceans beneath their icy crusts, kept warm not by the sun but by radioactive decay deep in their interiors. If true, they join a growing list of ocean worlds in the sol - Space Daily
item.description: Scientists think at least two of Uranus's moons — Titania and Oberon — may harbour liquid water oceans beneath their icy crusts, kept warm not by the sun but by radioactive decay deep in their interiors. If true, they join a growing list of ocean worlds in the sol  Space Daily

item.title: Astronauts returning from six-month missions describe a persistent 'observer' sensation — the feeling of watching their own lives from a half-step outside the frame, weeks after they're back on the ground - spacewar.com
item.description:
1. Astronauts returning from six-month missions describe a persistent 'observer' sensation — the feeling of watching their own lives from a half-step outside the frame, weeks after they're back on the ground  spacewar.com
  2. NASA debriefs and crew psychologists hear it often enough to treat it as normal readjustment: astronauts back from long missions describe observing their own lives rather than fully taking part in them  Space Daily
  3. Why the smell of rain feels overwhelming to astronauts after spending months in space, according to Nasa  The Times of India
  4. Astronauts returning from long stays in orbit say the smell of rain is almost too much to bear and a familiar street feels like something they are piloting rather than driving  Space Daily
  5. Astronauts who have seen the whole Earth at once describe the overview effect — every war and border and argument hanging in silence — and afterwards traffic and small talk can feel strangely far away, like the world seen through glass  Space Daily

item.title: JWST captures rare glimpse of early black hole growing inside network of young galaxies - Phys.org
item.description:
1. JWST captures rare glimpse of early black hole growing inside network of young galaxies  Phys.org
  2. James Webb Space Telescope discovers how black holes feed themselves  Space
  3. NASA’s James Webb catches a supermassive black hole feeding  ScienceDaily
  4. The James Webb Space Telescope spent nearly eight hours staring into NGC 4696, a galaxy 145 million light-years away, and watched a river of cool gas pour into an 800-light-year disk spinning at 600 kilometers per second, feeding the supermassive black  Space Daily
  5. JWST captures black hole recycling its own fuel  MSN

item.description: Scientists drill 33 feet into a vanishing Alpine glacier to recover a 2,000-year climate record revealing  The Times of India

item.title: "I could hardly believe what I was seeing. Suddenly out of nowhere the massive school of fish parted and a great white emerged from underneath…" - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description: "I could hardly believe what I was seeing. Suddenly out of nowhere the massive school of fish parted and a great white emerged from underneath…"  BBC Wildlife Magazine

item.title: Why crabs walk sideways instead of forwards – and the answer is stranger than you think - BBC Wildlife Magazine

item.title: A global molecular code for birth order and neuronal identity in Drosophila - Nature
item.description: A global molecular code for birth order and neuronal identity in Drosophila  Nature

item.title: The Largest Intact Meteorite on Earth Is Still Exactly Where It Landed. But Why No Crater? - ZME Science
item.description: The Largest Intact Meteorite on Earth Is Still Exactly Where It Landed. But Why No Crater?  ZME Science
